}}

The '''Cuba national football team''' ({{langx|es|Selección de fútbol de Cuba}}) represents [[Cuba]] in men's international [[Association football|football]], which is governed by the [[Football Association of Cuba|Asociación de Fútbol de Cuba]] ({{langx|en|Football Association of Cuba}}) founded in 1924. It has been an affiliate member of [[FIFA]] since 1932 and a founding affiliate member of [[CONCACAF]] since 1961. Regionally, it is an affiliate a member of [[Caribbean Football Union|CFU]] in the Caribbean Zone. It was a member of [[North American Football Confederation|NAFC]] (1946–1955) and later a member of [[Confederación Centroamericana y del Caribe de Fútbol|CCCF]] (1955–1961), the two predecessor confederations of CONCACAF. It was also a member of [[Panamerican Championship#Panamerican Football Confederation|PFC]], the former unified confederation of the Americas.

Cuba has participated once in the [[FIFA World Cup]] in [[1938 FIFA World Cup|1938]], becoming the first Caribbean team to play in the competition. It is one of twelve CONCACAF teams that have participated and also one of four CONCACAF teams to advanced to the knockout stage, reaching the quarter-finals.
In the round of 16, Cuba defeated [[Romania national football team|Romania]] in a replay, 2–1, after drawing against them 3–3. They were then eliminated in the quarter-finals by [[Sweden men's national football team|Sweden]], 8–0. Cuba has not returned to the World Cup since.

Cuba has participated twelve times in CONCACAF's premier continental competition, finishing fourth place in the [[1971 CONCACAF Championship]]. The team's best performance under the [[CONCACAF Gold Cup]] format was reaching the quarter-finals three times ([[2003 CONCACAF Gold Cup|2003]], [[2013 CONCACAF Gold Cup|2013]] and [[2015 CONCACAF Gold Cup|2015]]). It has participated three times in League A and once in League B of the [[CONCACAF Nations League]].
Regionally, the team finished as runners-up in the [[North American Football Confederation#NAFC Championship|NAFC Championship]] as hosts in [[1947 NAFC Championship|1947]] (organized by NAFC, the former North American confederation), it won the [[Caribbean Cup]] in [[2012 Caribbean Cup|2012]] (organized by CFU, the regional body for the Caribbean Zone), and one gold medal at the [[Football at the Central American and Caribbean Games|Central American and Caribbean Games]] in [[Football at the 1930 Central American and Caribbean Games|1930]].

==History==
===Early history===
Cuba played its first international football match on 16 March 1930 at [[1930 Central American and Caribbean Games|that year's Central American and Caribbean Games]]. They beat [[Jamaica national football team|Jamaica]] (then a British colony) 3–1 in Havana. Four days later, they beat [[Honduras national football team|Honduras]] 7–0.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesc/cuba-intres.html |title=Cuba&nbsp;– List of International Matches |publisher=Rsssf.com |access-date=2012-07-08 |archive-date=9 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220709082411/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesc/cuba-intres.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Cuba were managed by [[José Tapia (football manager)|José Tapia]], who remained in charge until after Cuba's [[1938 FIFA World Cup|1938 World Cup]] campaign.

Cuba's first World Cup qualification campaign was for the [[1934 FIFA World Cup|1934 World Cup]] in [[Italy]]. All of the CONCACAF entrants were placed in Group 11. The winner of a best-of-three tournament between the two weakest nations, Cuba and [[Haiti national football team|Haiti]] would produce a winner to play 1930 qualifiers [[Mexico national football team|Mexico]] in another round of best-of-three. The winner of that would play 1930 semi-finalists the [[United States men's national soccer team|United States]] of America for a place in the finals. All of the matches between Cuba and Haiti in the first round were staged at Parc Leconte in [[Port-au-Prince]], Haiti, on  28, 1 and 4 January–February 1934. Cuba won the first match 3–1 with Mario Lopez opening from a penalty and [[Hector Socorro]] scoring the second. The second match was a 1–1 draw with Lopez equalising in the 85th minute. In the final match, Cuba won 6–0 with two Lopez goals, one from Hector Socorro and one from his brother Francisco.

The second round against [[Mexico national football team|Mexico]] consisted of three matches at [[Parque Necaxa]] in [[Mexico City]]. Its first match, on 4 March 1934, saw Mexico go 3–0 up with a hat-trick by [[Dionisio Mejia]], his goals scored in the 12th, 14th and 16th minutes. Mario Lopez scored twice for Cuba with one in each half, but Mexico won 3–2. A week later, Mejia scored another hat-trick as Mexico won 5–0, and seven days later, Mexico won 4–1 after Lopez opened the scoring in the 15th minute. Mexico did not qualify, their 4–2 play-off defeat to the United States was held in Rome during the finals, as the two teams had forgotten to stage it earlier.

===1938–2000===
[[File:Sweden vs cuba 1938 world cup.jpg|thumb|left|Cuba v Sweden at the 1938 World Cup]]
The decision to stage the [[1938 FIFA World Cup|1938 World Cup]] in [[France]] was poorly received in the Americas, who had hoped for it to return to South America after the 1934 World Cup in Italy. All nations in South America except [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]] withdrew, and all CONCACAF nations except Cuba, thus the two qualified by default.

The tournament was held as a straight knock-out tournament of 16 nations. Cuba were drawn to play their first-ever World Cup finals match against [[Romania national football team|Romania]] (who were making their third finals appearance) at [[Stade Chapou|Stade du T.O.E.C.]] in [[Toulouse]], on 5 June 1938. [[Silviu Bindea]] put Romania ahead after 35 minutes and [[Hector Socorro]] equalised nine minutes later. With three minutes remaining, [[Tomás Fernández (footballer, born 1915)|Tomás Fernández]] gave Cuba the lead, but within a minute [[Iuliu Baratky]] forced extra time with a Romanian equaliser. Romania went 3–2 up in extra-time by [[Ștefan Dobay]]'s goal on 105 minutes, but [[Juan Tuñas]] equalised for Cuba with three minutes of extra-time remaining.

The replay was held at the same stadium, on 9 June. This was at the same time as [[Switzerland national football team|Switzerland]]'s 4–2 replay win over [[Germany national football team|Germany]]. Dobay put Romania 1–0 up at half-time with a 35th-minute goal, but in the second half Cuba equalised through Socorro in the 51st minute. Six minutes later, Carlos Oliviera scored the winning goal and it ended 2–1.
In the quarter-final, Cuba lost 8–0 to [[Sweden men's national football team|Sweden]] at [[Stade du Fort Carre]] in Antibes, on 12 June. Sweden's [[Tore Keller]] and [[Gustav Wetterström]] each scored hat-tricks.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/05/02/world/americas/soccer-gains-in-cuba-where-baseball-is-king.htm|title=Baseball in Their Veins, but a New Ball at Their Feet|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=2012-07-08}}</ref>

The [[NAFC Championship 1949]] served as CONCACAF's qualification group for the [[1950 FIFA World Cup|1950 World Cup]] in [[Brazil]]. Cuba, the United States of America and Mexico played each other twice in a tournament held in [[Mexico City]] in September 1949. The top two would qualify. Cuba came third and did not qualify, their only point was gained from their second match, a 1–1 draw against the United States on 14 September. Cuba did not compete in World Cup qualification again until 1966, already under [[Fidel Castro|Castro]]'s regime. They returned to participation in qualification for [[1978 FIFA World Cup|1978]], but the 1982 qualifiers represented a significant breakthrough- Cuba reached the final round of qualifying, and were only two points short of reaching the [[1982 FIFA World Cup|1982 World Cup]]. In recent years, Cuban football has seen an improvement in results.

===2000–present===

[[File:CubaUSACGC2015 (2) (Removed watermark).jpg |thumb|500px|The national football team of Cuba in 2015]]
They reached the quarter-finals of the [[2003 CONCACAF Gold Cup|2003 Gold Cup]] (where they were beaten by the United States) by defeating Canada 2–0 in the Group stage. During the [[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006 World Cup]] [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONCACAF)|qualifiers]], Cuba faced [[Costa Rica national football team|Costa Rica]] and were only eliminated on away goals. They held Costa Rica to a draw in Havana 2–2 and later battled it out for a 1–1 draw in Costa Rica.

During the [[2010 FIFA World Cup|2010 World Cup]] [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONCACAF)|qualifiers]], Cuba faced [[Antigua and Barbuda national football team|Antigua and Barbuda]] and the match ended in a 3–3 draw. Later in Pedro Marreo, Cuba won 4–1 to advance to the semi-final round of the CONCACAF World Cup qualifiers. Cuba was with the United States, [[Trinidad and Tobago national football team|Trinidad and Tobago]] and [[Guatemala national football team|Guatemala]]. Cuba finished in the fourth place with only one victory against Guatemala 2–1 with a goal scored by Aliannis Urgellés.
They finished in third place in the [[2010 Caribbean Cup]] to take a place in the [[2011 CONCACAF Gold Cup|2011 Gold Cup]]. In 2012, Cuba won the [[2012 Caribbean Cup|Caribbean Cup]] for the first time.

For the [[2014 FIFA World Cup|2014 World Cup]], the Cuban team qualified directly to the Third round as one of the six highest ranked teams and were placed in Group C with [[Honduras national football team|Honduras]], [[Panama national football team|Panama]], and [[Canada men's national soccer team|Canada]]. Although the Cuban team had several close games, they ended their qualification process with one draw and five defeats (losing home and away to Canada and Honduras and drawing to Panama in Havana in their final game after losing in [[Panama City]]). Their only goal of the qualifying campaign came from Alberto Gomes against Panama in the final game of the group stage.

==Defection and economic migration by Cuban athletes==
{{details|List of Cuban football players who have defected to the United States}}

As well as Cuban athletes in other sports, a number of football players have made the move to the United States in recent years. During the [[2002 CONCACAF Gold Cup|2002 Gold Cup]] in [[Los Angeles]], two Cuban players [[Rey Ángel Martínez]] and [[Alberto Delgado Pérez|Alberto Delgado]] chose to remain in the United States. Striker [[Maykel Galindo]] did so during the [[2005 CONCACAF Gold Cup|2005 Gold Cup]]. Two more, [[Osvaldo Alonso]] and [[Lester More]] did so during the [[2007 CONCACAF Gold Cup|2007 Gold Cup]].

In 2008, defections occurred during two separate tournaments held in the United States. In March, seven players from the U-23 national football, including [[Yeniel Bermúdez]], [[Yordany Álvarez]] and [[Yendry Díaz]] defected during the 2008 CONCACAF Olympic Qualifying tournament while the team was based in Tampa, FL. In October, two days before the country's World Cup Qualifier versus the US, [[Reynier Alcántara]] and [[Pedro Faife]] walked away from the team's hotel near Washington, D.C.

During the [[2011 CONCACAF Gold Cup]], [[Yosniel Mesa]] defected while the team was in [[Charlotte, North Carolina]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Cuba's Yosniel Mesa defects|url=https://www.espn.com/sports/soccer/news/_/id/6655128/cuba-yosniel-mesa-defects-gold-cup-tourney|agency=Associated Press|publisher=ESPN|access-date=13 June 2011|archive-date=12 July 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150712053727/http://espn.go.com/sports/soccer/news/_/id/6655128/cuba-yosniel-mesa-defects-gold-cup-tourney|url-status=live}}</ref> During the [[2015 Gold Cup]], forward [[Keiler García]] defected to the United States before the team's first match against [[Mexico national football team|Mexico]] in [[Chicago]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Keilen García, el premio-castigo de la libertad|url=http://www.espn.com.mx/blogs/index?entryID=2416610&name=rafa_ramos|publisher=ESPN Deportes|access-date=9 July 2015|archive-date=10 July 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150710202826/http://www.espn.com.mx/blogs/index?entryID=2416610&name=rafa_ramos|url-status=live}}</ref> In September 2019, five players ([[Yordan Santa Cruz]], [[Andy Baquero]], [[David Urgelles]], [[Orlendis Benítez]] and [[Alejandro Portal]]) defected to Canada during the [[2019–20 CONCACAF Nations League]] before and after a match against [[Canada men's national soccer team|Canada]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.elnuevoherald.com/deportes/futbol/article234861267.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190921204616/https://www.elnuevoherald.com/deportes/futbol/article234861267.html|date=September 8, 2019|archive-date=September 21, 2019|url-status=live|title=Tres cubanos más escapan en torneo de CONCACAF y ya suman cinco los que abandonan el equipo|first1=Jorge|last1=Ebro|first2=Pedro J.|last2=González|language=es|trans-title=Three more Cubans escape in the CONCACAF tournament and there are already five who leave the team|work=[[El Nuevo Herald]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.mlssoccer.com/news/report-three-cuba-national-team-players-defect-after-canada-loss|title=Report: Five Cuba national team players defect during Nations League|date=September 9, 2019|first=Alicia|last=Rodriguez|work=[[Major League Soccer]]|access-date=4 July 2022|archive-date=3 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220703121636/https://www.mlssoccer.com/news/report-three-cuba-national-team-players-defect-after-canada-loss|url-status=live}}</ref>

During the [[2023 CONCACAF Gold Cup]], four players ([[Roberney Caballero]], [[Denilson Milanés]], [[Neisser Sandó]] and [[Jassael Herrera]]) defected while the team was preparing to travel from [[Miami]] to [[Houston]] for their last two group stage matches.<ref>{{cite news |date=29 June 2023 |title=Cuatro futbolistas de la selección de Cuba escaparon tras el primer partido de la Copa Oro en Miami |language=es |work=[[Infobae]] |url=https://www.infobae.com/america/america-latina/2023/06/29/cuatro-futbolistas-de-la-seleccion-de-cuba-escaparon-tras-el-primer-partido-de-la-copa-oro-en-miami/ |access-date=29 June 2023}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-06-28 |title=Reportes: Cuatro futbolistas abandonaron la concentración de Cuba en Miami durante la Copa Oro |url=https://espndeportes.espn.com/futbol/guatemala/nota/_/id/12244649/cuatro-futbolistas-abandonaron-concentracion-cuba-miami-durante-copa-oro |access-date=2023-07-09 |website=[[ESPN Deportes]] |language=es}}</ref> Another player, [[Sandy Sánchez]], also defected after the last group stage game against Canada,<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-07-07 |title=Cinque giocatori cubani hanno abbandonato la Nazionale durante la Gold Cup, e ora sono irreperibili |url=https://www.rivistaundici.com/2023/07/07/gold-cup-cuba/ |access-date=2023-07-09 |website=Rivista Undici |language=it-IT}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-07-05 |title=Sandy Sánchez, portero de Cuba y Pantoja, desertó después de Copa Oro |url=https://espndeportes.espn.com/futbol/norte-centro-america/nota/_/id/12274580/sandy-sanchez-portero-seleccion-cubana-atletico-pantoja-deserto-copa-oro |access-date=2023-07-09 |website=[[ESPN Deportes]] |language=es}}</ref> with a member of the medical staff reportedly following suit.<ref name=":0" />

| team2      = {{fb|CUB}}

}}

| goals2     = [[Dairon Reyes|D. Reyes]] {{goal|51}}

| stadium    = [[Estadio Cibao FC]]
| attendance = 
| referee    = Roger Mélendez ([[Nicaraguan Football Federation|Nicaragua]])
| result     = D
}}

==Coaching history==
{{div col|colwidth=22em}}
*{{flagicon|Cuba|1902}} [[José Tapia (football manager)|José Tapia]] (1930–1934)
*{{flagicon|United States|1912}} [[Gavin Newton]] (1934–1935)
*{{flagicon|Cuba|1902}} [[José Tapia (football manager)|José Tapia]] (1935–1938)
*{{flagicon|Cuba|1902}} [[Marcelino Minsal]] (1947–1949)
*{{flagicon|Czechoslovakia}} [[František Churda]] (1963–1964)
*{{flagicon|Hungary}} [[Karoly Kósa]] (1966)
*{{flagicon|Hungary}} [[László Mohácsi]] (1967)
*{{flagicon|North Korea|1948}} [[Kim Yong-ha]] (1970–1971)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Sergio Padrón]] (1976)
*{{flagicon|Hungary}} [[Tibor Ivanics]] (1980–1981)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Roberto Hernández (Cuban footballer)|Roberto Hernández]] (1985–1988)
*{{flagicon|Italy}} [[Giovanni Campari]] (1990–1996)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[William Bennett Barracks|William Bennett]] (1996–2000)
*{{flagicon|Peru}} [[Miguel Company]] (2000–2004)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Luis Armelio Garcia]] (2004–2005)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Raúl González (football manager, born 1968)|Raúl González]] (2006–2007)
*{{flagicon|Germany}} [[Reinhold Fanz]] (2008)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Raúl González (football manager, born 1968)|Raúl González]] (2008–2012)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Chandler González]] (2012)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Walter Benítez (manager)|Walter Benítez]] (2012–2015)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Raúl González (football manager, born 1968)|Raúl González]] (2015–2016)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Julio Valero]] (2016)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Raúl Mederos]] (2016–2019)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Pablo Elier Sánchez]] (2019–2023)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Yunielys Castillo]] (2023–2025)
*{{flagicon|Cuba}} [[Pedro Pablo Pereira]] (2025-Present)
{{div col end}}

==Players==
===Current squad===
The following players were called up for the [[2025–26 CONCACAF Series]] matches against [[Martinique national football team|Martinique]] and [[Dominican Republic national football team|Dominican Republic]] on 26 and 29 March 2026.<ref>{{cite web |title=Summoned to the March window of the Concacaf Friendly Series |url=https://www.facebook.com/permalink.php?story_fbid=pfbid0UNqmsLZdqTQ3p2Qt3RT1iSK9Fp54qq8qCek49j9HEoceSKbiPVsyEa74hLAEenFdl&id=100087098190157&rdid=8sTXMGO2ViyqZEWM# |website=Facebook |publisher=Cuban Football Association}}</ref>
<br>''Caps and goals correct as of 30 March 2026, after the match against [[Dominican Republic national football team|Dominican Republic]].''

{{nat fs g start}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Ismel Morgado]]|age={{Bda|df=yes|2003|5|31}}|caps=6|goals=0|club=[[ART Municipal Jalapa]]|clubnat=NCA}}

{{nat fs break}}

{{nat fs end}}

===Recent call-ups=== 
The following players have also been called up to the Cuba squad within the last twelve months.
{{nat fs r start}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Raiko Arozarena]]|age={{Bda|df=yes|1997|3|27}}|caps=15|goals=0|club=[[New Mexico United]]|clubnat=USA|latest=v. {{fb|BER}}, 10 June 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Ricardo Polo]]|age={{Bda|df=yes|2005|5|14}}|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[Club Sport Sebaco|Sébaco]]|clubnat=NIC|latest=v. {{fb|BER}}, 10 June 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Rey Rodríguez]]|age={{birth date and age|2003|1|15|df=y}}|caps=10|goals=0|club=[[FC San Francisco Masachapa|Masachapa]]|clubnat=NCA|latest=v. {{fb|VCT}}, 8 October 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Maikel Reyes]]|age={{Bda|df=yes|1993|3|4}}|caps=49|goals=13|club=[[Managua FC|Managua]]|clubnat=NCA|latest=v. {{fb|MTQ}}, 15 November 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Willian Pozo-Venta]]|age={{Bda|df=yes|1997|8|27}}|caps=24|goals=4|club=[[Grorud IL|Grorud]]|clubnat=NOR|latest=v. {{fb|BER}}, 10 June 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Onel Hernández]]|age={{Bda|df=yes|1993|2|1}}|caps=14|goals=4|club=[[Port Vale F.C.|Port Vale]]|clubnat=ENG|latest=v. {{fb|BER}}, 10 June 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Martín Rodríguez (footballer born 2005)|Martín Rodríguez]]|age={{Bda|df=yes|2005|8|31}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[Manhattan Jaspers men's soccer|Manhattan Jaspers]]|clubnat=USA|latest=v. {{fb|BER}}, 10 June 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
<sup>INJ</sup> Player withdrew from the squad due to an injury.<br>
<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad.<br>
<sup>RET</sup> Player retired from the national team.<br>
<sup>SUS</sup> Player is serving suspension.<br>
<sup>WD</sup> Player withdrew from the squad due to non-injury issue.<br>
{{nat fs end}}

==Player records==
{{updated|10 June 2026}}<ref>{{cite web |last1=Passo Alpuin |first1=Luis Fernando |title=Cuba - Record International Players |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/cuba-recintlp.html |website=RSSSF |access-date=2 February 2023 |archive-date=19 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230319111240/https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/cuba-recintlp.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Cuba''

==Honours==
===Regional===
* '''[[North American Football Confederation#NAFC Championship|NAFC Championship]]'''<sup>'''1'''</sup>
** {{silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[1947 NAFC Championship|1947]]
** {{bronze3}} Third place (1): [[1949 NAFC Championship|1949]]
*'''[[Caribbean Cup]]'''
** {{gold1}} '''Champions (1):''' [[2012 Caribbean Cup|2012]]<ref>{{cite web|url=https://int.soccerway.com/matches/2012/12/17/nc-america/caribbean-cup/cuba/trinidad-and-tobago/1414017/|title=Cuba 1 Trinidad & Tobago 0|date=16 December 2012|work=Soccerway|access-date=17 December 2012|archive-date=16 February 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130216041353/http://int.soccerway.com/matches/2012/12/17/nc-america/caribbean-cup/cuba/trinidad-and-tobago/1414017/|url-status=live}}</ref>
** {{silver2}} Runners-up (3): [[1996 Caribbean Cup|1996]], [[1999 Caribbean Cup|1999]], [[2005 Caribbean Cup|2005]]
** {{bronze3}} Third place (3): [[1995 Caribbean Cup|1995]], [[2007 Caribbean Cup|2007]], [[2010 Caribbean Cup|2010]]
* '''[[Football at the Central American and Caribbean Games|Central American and Caribbean Games]]'''
** {{gold1}} '''Gold medal (1)''': [[Football at the 1930 Central American and Caribbean Games|1930]]

===Summary===
{| class="wikitable" width=30% style="font-size:90%; text-align:center;"
!Competition !!{{gold1}} !!{{silver2}} !!{{bronze3}} !!Total
|-
|align=left|[[North American Football Confederation#NAFC Championship|NAFC Championship]]<sup>'''1'''</sup>
|0||1||1||2
|-
!Total!!0!!1!!1!!2
|}
;Notes:
# Official regional competition organized by [[North American Football Confederation|NAFC]]. It was a predecessor confederation of CONCACAF, affiliated with FIFA as the former governing body of football in North America, from 1946 to 1961.
